subject: civic
learning_objectives: Identify and explain the types of values

sections.heading: Meaning of Values
sections.content: Values are things that are important to us. Values are things we love, admire or cherish. Values are ideas and beliefs we hold as special. Values are traits or qualities that are considered good. Values are the motive power behind purposeful actions. Values are the ends to which we act. Without them, life would be impossible. Value specifies a relationship between a person and a goal. The goal to which one aims is called the 'value' but the relationship is always required. Your values are made of everything that has happened to your life. They include influences from your parents and family, your religious affiliation, your friends and playmates, your education, your reading, etc.

sections.heading: Types of Values
sections.content:
Everything has a price and one has to pay the right price to get one's needs satisfied. We value things based on our needs and the needs depend on the basis of our value system. According to Awdhesh K. Singh, human values can be divided into six groups:

a. Individualistic Values: Every human being values himself or herself over everything in the world. The modern world has been moving more and more towards individualistic values where the interest of the individual is considered to be right and must be protected. Everybody supports freedom because he or she has the right to decide what is good for himself or herself. Examples include adoration, affection, beauty, brilliance, cleanliness, freedom, fun, honesty, discipline, eagerness, enjoyment, contentment, respect, self-control, etc.

b. Family Values: Every child cannot survive on his own. The support of the family is needed for the growth of the child. Every member of the family lives like a unit and supports a new born child till he becomes strong enough. The family is considered as the basic unit of every society. The entire family value is maintained by custom, tradition and trust. Examples include wealth, wisdom, unity, fame, neatness, respect, meekness, etc.

c. Professional Values: In government and non-government organisations, people work and perform functions, and so a set of values is needed to keep them going. The workers develop these values in their professions. The values of a policeman, for example, are different from those of a medical doctor or a teacher. Examples include achievement, bravery, calmness, courtesy, discipline, integrity, punctuality, etc.

d. National Values: The world today is divided into a number of countries, each sovereign and independent. The values of the country are often produced in laws or in the constitution. These laws seek to grant equality and justice to all citizens. The violation of the country's values is treated as criminal acts which are punished by the government. Nigeria's values include discipline, integrity, patriotism, religious tolerance, the national flag, national anthem, national pledge, and the currency.

e. Moral Values: Moral values are the standards of good and evil which govern an individual's behaviour and choices. When you separate right from wrong, good from bad, you are talking about morals. Moral values are passed on from one generation to another by tradition and are often more powerful than the law in keeping a person on the right path. Examples include friendliness, kindness, bravery, obedience, dignity, trustworthiness, honesty, loyalty, care, etc.

f. Spiritual Values: Spiritual values are values not created by man but by God. They do not change while human values can. Spiritual values unite all human beings in this world and cannot be eliminated from man. Examples include love, compassion, justice, truth, holiness, faith, devotion, trust, thankfulness, visions, and spirituality.

sections.heading: Importance of Values
sections.content:
a. Support and Direction: Values provide the essential needs of a person and the society. Without values, it will be difficult to take decisions. For example, if a person's value is diligence, he will take decisions that relate to this value.

b. Attitudes and Behaviours: Values influence a person's attitudes and behaviours. They describe the special way people in an organisation work and relate to one another. Values also describe the personality or character of a person. For example, if your value is cleanliness, you will try to be neat always.

c. Priorities: Values are priorities that tell us how to spend our limited resources. When we discover our priorities or basic needs, time can be allocated to them and wastage will be reduced.

d. Easy Identification: The values a person holds assist in determining the position of that person on any matter. For example, if a person is honest, sincere, and reliable, it is easy to know their stand on cases of bribery and corruption.
